fuzzy_prd:
We need a configurable text-conversion engine that turns Markdown source into clean HTML fragments. Authors write content in plain-text shorthand — headings, lists, emphasis, code blocks, links, images, tables, and so on — and the engine produces the corresponding HTML. The active set of constructs should be configurable per call: a caller can say 'only enable these features', 'add these on top of the defaults', or 'turn these off', so it's possible to restrict what gets processed for a given document — for example, let only headings and paragraphs through, or disable raw HTML passthrough for untrusted input. One special flag controls whether inline markup inside headings gets processed at all. The default set includes all the common constructs but deliberately excludes raw HTML passthrough and the math block, which must be explicitly turned on. When a paragraph contains multiple lines of text they should be joined with a single space and the block should end with a trailing space before its closing tag — handle that consistently the way the rest of the block rules handle running text. One small follow-up from the questions that came in: for plain running text, a paragraph keeps a single trailing space right before

, and if the text for that one paragraph spans multiple lines, those adjacent lines get joined with a single space. So 'line1\nline2\n' produces '

line1 line2

'. Also confirming the defaults and names exactly: raw HTML passthrough ('html') and the math/latex block ('latex_block') are OFF by default. All other constructs are on by default. Valid feature names are: break_line, checklist, code_block, emphasis, headline, horizontal_line, html, image, inline_code, italic, link, ordered_list, paragraph, quote, strikethrough, strong, table, unordered_list, latex_block. And for checklist rendering, the outer list is still a
    but it needs class="checklist". Each item should be wrapped in . An unchecked item produces . A checked item (bracket with 'x') produces .

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: main(stdin: JSON object with markdown/config) -> stdout rendered HTML

trigger_keywords: how many hash marks are allowed for a heading
what happens with more than six hash marks
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
One through six hash marks followed by a space are valid headings. Seven or more hash marks are NOT a valid heading; such a line falls through to paragraph rendering: '####### a' becomes '

####### a

'.

trigger_keywords: what language label does a fenced code block produce
how is the language annotation on a code block emitted
where does the language class appear in the code block output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
Text following the opening triple-backtick fence on the same line is treated as a language label and emitted as a class on the 
 element: '
...'. Without a label: '
\nsome code\n
'.

Full Markdown renderer surface: paragraphs and hard line breaks, italic/emphasis/strong/strikethrough, inline code protection, links/images, unordered/ordered/nested lists, task checklists, block quotes, horizontal rules, tables, raw HTML blocks, math/fenced code blocks with labels, headings including max-level rules and inline parsing, and feature toggles/configuration.

The adapter converts the markdown string using config flags. Public_test_cases are authoritative for exact HTML tags, nesting, escaping, newlines, checkbox attributes, table layout, raw HTML preservation, and code-language labels.

The embedded public_test_cases are part of oracle_data and are authoritative for exact input shapes, action/operation names, stdout line order, whitespace, error categories, and deterministic formatting. When prose and expected_output differ, expected_output is the pass-critical contract.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: handle that consistently the way the rest of the block rules handle running text
context_pointers.actual_meaning:
In the maddy C++ parser, the paragraph rule (ParagraphParser) joins consecutive non-blank lines with a single space and appends a trailing space before

. This same trailing-space convention applies across all block-level text containers.

oracle_data.fallback_response: Please refer to the start.md specification. The engine takes a 'markdown' field and optional 'config' object with 'only', 'enable', 'disable', and 'headline_inline_parsing' fields.

feature10_table.json.description: Render a delimited table into table markup. A region opened and closed by table fence markers contains a header row, a body, and optionally a footer, with cells separated by a vertical bar and row groups separated by a dash-only divider row. The first row group becomes the table head, the next becomes the body, and a final group becomes the footer; inline markup inside cells is still applied.

input.markdown:
|table>
Left header|middle header|last header
- | - | -
cell 1|cell 2|cell 3
cell 4|cell 5|cell 6
- | - | -
foot a|foot b|foot c
|<table

feature11_1_full_document.json.description: Convert a complete multi-feature document with the default feature set enabled. The input mixes headings, paragraphs, nested lists, fenced code, a block quote containing a list, inline markup, a link, an image, a thematic break, a checklist, and a table; the output is the concatenation of each block's HTML in document order.

input.markdown: # This is a test This should result in a praragraph it's that simple. * an *unordered* list * with some **hierarchy** 1. and an _ordered_ * list * directly * inside ``` var c = 'blub'; ``` > A Quote > > With some ~~text~~ blocks inside > > * even a list > * should be > * possible > And well `inline code` should also work. ## Another Headline And not to forget [link to progsource](http://progsource.de) should work. And well - let's see how an image would be shown: ![an image](http://progsource.de/img/progsource.png) --- ### and more headlines - [ ] how - [ ] about - [ ] a - [x] nice - [x] check - [ ] list #### even a table |table> Left header|middle header|last header - | - | - cell 1|cell **2**|cell 3 cell 4|cell 5|cell 6 - | - | - foot a|foot b|foot c |

feature11_2_disabled_features.json.description: Convert the same complete document but with the single-underscore emphasis handling turned off and raw inline HTML pass-through turned on. With emphasis disabled, underscore spans are left as literal text; with HTML pass-through enabled, a standalone HTML line is emitted as-is instead of being wrapped in a paragraph.

feature11_3_minimal_feature_set.json.description: Convert the same complete document but with only the bold and emphasis inline handlers enabled and every block-level handler disabled. No block structure is produced; the document is flattened so each source line is separated by a line-break element, while doubled-marker spans still become bold and single-underscore spans still become emphasis.

feature11_4_headline_inline.json.description: Control whether inline markup inside a heading is processed. By default a heading's text is run through inline handling, so a doubled-marker span inside the heading becomes bold; when inline processing inside headings is turned off, the heading text is emitted literally with its markers intact.

feature12_html_passthrough.json.description: Emit raw HTML blocks unchanged when HTML pass-through is enabled. With this handler enabled, a line that begins with an HTML tag starts a raw block whose lines are passed through verbatim (joined with spaces across multiple lines), while ordinary text outside such blocks is still wrapped as a paragraph.

input.markdown:
some text in a paragraph
some HTML
more HTML

cases.expected_output:
some text in a paragraph

some HTML
more HTML

feature13_latex_block.json.description: Pass a math block through verbatim when the math handler is enabled. A line opened and closed with a doubled dollar delimiter on the same line is emitted exactly as written (including the delimiters) followed by a newline, so a downstream math renderer can process it; the handler is off in the default feature set and must be explicitly enabled.

feature14_line_break.json.description: Convert an embedded carriage return into a line-break element. Within a single logical line, a carriage-return character is replaced by a line-break element so the surrounding text is split across visual lines inside the same block.

input.markdown: Test the texttest text to checkcheck testing to text.

cases.expected_output:
Test the text
test text to check
check testing to text.

feature1_headings.json.description: Convert ATX-style heading lines into HTML heading elements. A line beginning with one through six hash marks followed by a space and text becomes the correspondingly ranked heading element wrapping that text; a run of more than six hash marks is not a valid heading and the line is rendered as ordinary paragraph text instead.

feature2_paragraphs.json.description: Group consecutive non-blank text lines into a single paragraph element. Successive lines that are not part of any other block are joined with a single space between them and wrapped as one paragraph; the joined text carries a trailing space before the closing tag.

input.markdown:
Some text
and some other text

feature3_1_italic.json.description: Render single-asterisk spans as italic. Text enclosed in single asterisks within a line becomes an italic element around the enclosed text, leaving surrounding text untouched. Multiple such spans on one line are each converted.

feature3_2_emphasis.json.description: Render single-underscore spans as emphasis. Text enclosed in single underscores within a line becomes an emphasis element around the enclosed text. Spans located inside an inline-code region are protected and left verbatim rather than being converted.

feature3_3_strong.json.description: Render double-asterisk or double-underscore spans as strong. A span wrapped in a doubled marker becomes a strong element, while a span wrapped in only a single marker is left for the lighter emphasis/italic handling and not promoted to strong. When a line also contains an inline-code region, strong promotion on that line is suppressed so code spans keep their literal markers.

feature3_4_strikethrough.json.description: Render double-tilde spans as struck-through text. Text enclosed in doubled tildes within a line becomes a strikethrough element around the enclosed text. Spans inside an inline-code region are protected and left verbatim.

feature3_5_inline_code.json.description: Render single-backtick spans as inline code. Text enclosed in single backticks within a line becomes an inline code element around the enclosed text; multiple such spans on one line are each converted.

feature4_links.json.description: Convert inline link syntax into anchor elements. A bracketed label immediately followed by a parenthesized target becomes an anchor whose visible text is the label and whose target is the parenthesized value. Several links may appear on one line, and a target value may itself contain spaces and parentheses without prematurely terminating the link.

input.markdown: (This is a [link](/ABC/some file) (the URL will include this).)

cases.expected_output: (This is a link (the URL will include this).)

feature5_images.json.description: Convert inline image syntax into image elements. A bracketed label preceded by an exclamation mark and immediately followed by a parenthesized target becomes an image element whose source is the parenthesized value and whose alternate text is the label. Several images may appear on one line and are each converted.

feature6_1_unordered_list.json.description: Convert bullet lists into unordered list markup. Consecutive lines that begin with a bullet marker (any of the common dash, asterisk, or plus markers) become list items inside an unordered list. Deeper indentation opens a nested unordered list inside the current item, and items at the same indentation continue the same list regardless of which bullet character was used.

input.markdown:
* a
* b
- c
- d
+ e
+ f
* g

* a
  * d
  * e
* b
  * c
  + x
  + y
  - z

feature6_2_ordered_list.json.description: Convert numbered lists into ordered list markup. Consecutive lines that begin with a number followed by a period and a space become items of an ordered list; the actual numeric values are not preserved, only the ordering. Deeper indentation opens a nested list, and a mixed continuation that switches bullet style still extends the current list.

feature6_3_checklist.json.description: Convert task-list lines into a checkbox list. Lines beginning with a bullet marker followed by a bracketed flag become list items rendered with a checkbox input inside a label; an empty bracket yields an unchecked box and a filled bracket yields a checked box. The enclosing list carries a checklist class, and deeper indentation nests a further checklist inside the parent item.

feature7_code_block.json.description: Render fenced code blocks verbatim. A run of lines delimited by triple-backtick fences becomes a preformatted code block whose inner text is preserved exactly, including line breaks. Any text following the opening fence on the same line is treated as a language label and emitted as a class on the preformatted element.

cases.expected_output:
some code
some other code

feature8_block_quote.json.description: Render quoted lines as a block quote. Consecutive lines beginning with a greater-than marker form a block quote; a marker-only line acts as a blank separator that splits the quoted content into separate paragraphs inside the quote. Inline markup within the quoted text is still applied.

feature9_horizontal_rule.json.description: Render a horizontal rule from a dashes-only line. A line consisting of exactly three dashes becomes a thematic-break element. A line that contains three dashes but also extra characters such as a trailing space is not a rule and is rendered as ordinary paragraph text.
